questions.question: Which two systems in the human body jointly coordinate and integrate all the activities of the organs so that they function in a synchronised fashion?
questions.explanation: In our body the neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and integrate all the activities of the organs so that they function in a synchronised fashion.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The neural system of all animals is composed of highly specialised cells called:
questions.explanation: The neural system of all animals is composed of highly specialised cells called neurons which can detect, receive and transmit different kinds of stimuli.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: How is the neural system organised in lower invertebrates like Hydra?
options: It consists of a well-developed brain and spinal cord.
It is composed of a network of neurons.
It contains a brain along with a number of ganglia.
It consists only of sensory receptors without neurons.
questions.explanation: The neural organisation is very simple in lower invertebrates. For example, in Hydra it is composed of a network of neurons.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The human neural system is broadly divided into two parts, which are:
options: Central neural system (CNS) and Peripheral neural system (PNS)
questions.explanation: The human neural system is divided into two parts: (i) the central neural system (CNS) (ii) the peripheral neural system (PNS).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In the peripheral neural system (PNS), what is the function of afferent nerve fibres?
options: They transmit impulses from the CNS to the peripheral tissues.
They transmit impulses from tissues/organs to the CNS.
They interconnect different regions of the brain.
They connect the somatic neural system to the autonomic neural system.
questions.explanation: The afferent nerve fibres transmit impulses from tissues/organs to the CNS and the efferent fibres transmit regulatory impulses from the CNS to the concerned peripheral tissues/organs.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which division of the peripheral neural system relays impulses from the CNS to skeletal muscles?
questions.explanation: The somatic neural system relays impulses from the CNS to skeletal muscles while the autonomic neural system transmits impulses from the CNS to the involuntary organs and smooth muscles of the body.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The autonomic neural system is further classified into:
questions.explanation: The autonomic neural system is further classified into sympathetic neural system and parasympathetic neural system.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: A neuron is a microscopic structure composed of three major parts. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.explanation: A neuron is a microscopic structure composed of three major parts, namely, cell body, dendrites and axon.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What are the granular bodies found in the cell body and dendrites of a neuron called?
questions.explanation: The cell body contains cytoplasm with typical cell organelles and certain granular bodies called Nissl's granules. Short fibres which branch repeatedly... also contain Nissl's granules and are called dendrites.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Each branch at the distal end of an axon terminates as a bulb-like structure called a:
questions.explanation: Each branch terminates as a bulb-like structure called synaptic knob which possess synaptic vesicles containing chemicals called neurotransmitters.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Where are multipolar neurons (neurons with one axon and two or more dendrites) typically found?
questions.explanation: Based on the number of axon and dendrites, the neurons are divided into three types, i.e., multipolar (with one axon and two or more dendrites; found in the cerebral cortex)...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Bipolar neurons, which possess one axon and one dendrite, are characteristically found in the:
questions.explanation: ...bipolar (with one axon and one dendrite, found in the retina of eye)...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The gaps between two adjacent myelin sheaths on an axon are called:
questions.explanation: The myelinated nerve fibres are enveloped with Schwann cells, which form a myelin sheath around the axon. The gaps between two adjacent myelin sheaths are called nodes of Ranvier.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Unmyelinated nerve fibres, which are enclosed by a Schwann cell that does not form a myelin sheath, are commonly found in:
options: The white matter of the brain
questions.explanation: Unmyelinated nerve fibre is enclosed by a Schwann cell that does not form a myelin sheath around the axon, and is commonly found in autonomous and the somatic neural systems.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: When a neuron is resting (not conducting any impulse), its axonal membrane is:
options: Comparatively more permeable to sodium ions (Na+) and nearly impermeable to potassium ions (K+)
Equally permeable to both sodium (Na+) and potassium (K+) ions
Comparatively more permeable to potassium ions (K+) and nearly impermeable to sodium ions (Na+)
questions.explanation: When a neuron is not conducting any impulse, i.e., resting, the axonal membrane is comparatively more permeable to potassium ions (K+) and nearly impermeable to sodium ions (Na+).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: During the resting state of a neuron, the axoplasm inside the axon contains:
options: High concentration of K+ and negatively charged proteins, and low concentration of Na+
High concentration of Na+ and negatively charged proteins, and low concentration of K+
Low concentration of K+ and negatively charged proteins, and high concentration of Na+
Equal concentrations of K+ and Na+
questions.explanation: Consequently, the axoplasm inside the axon contains high concentration of K+ and negatively charged proteins and low concentration of Na+.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The sodium-potassium pump maintains the ionic gradients across the resting membrane by actively transporting:
options: 3 Na+ outwards for 2 K+ into the cell
questions.explanation: These ionic gradients across the resting membrane are maintained by the active transport of ions by the sodium-potassium pump which transports 3 Na+ outwards for 2 K+ into the cell.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The electrical potential difference across the resting plasma membrane is known as the:
questions.explanation: As a result, the outer surface of the axonal membrane possesses a positive charge while its inner surface becomes negatively charged and, therefore is, polarised. The electrical potential difference across the resting plasma membrane is called as the resting potential.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: When a stimulus is applied to a site on a polarised membrane, the membrane at that site becomes freely permeable to:
questions.explanation: When a stimulus is applied at a site... on the polarised membrane, the membrane at the site A becomes freely permeable to Na+.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The rapid influx of Na+ following a stimulus leads to the reversal of polarity at that site, a process known as:
questions.explanation: This leads to a rapid influx of Na+ followed by the reversal of the polarity at that site... The polarity of the membrane at the site A is thus reversed and hence depolarised.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The electrical potential difference across the plasma membrane at a depolarised site is called the:
questions.explanation: The electrical potential difference across the plasma membrane at the site A is called the action potential, which is in fact termed as a nerve impulse.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: During the conduction of a nerve impulse, how does the current flow on the inner surface of the axon?
options: From the unexcited site to the excited site
From the excited site (site A) to the adjacent unexcited site (site B)
It does not flow on the inner surface
It flows evenly in both directions simultaneously
questions.explanation: As a result, a current flows on the inner surface from site A to site B. On the outer surface current flows from site B to site A to complete the circuit of current flow.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What process quickly follows the stimulus-induced permeability to Na+, helping to restore the resting potential?
options: A rise in permeability to K+, which diffuses outside the membrane
A massive influx of Cl- ions
The continuous pumping of Na+ into the cell
The closure of all ion channels indefinitely
questions.explanation: It is quickly followed by a rise in permeability to K+. Within a fraction of a second, K+ diffuses outside the membrane and restores the resting potential of the membrane...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: At which type of synapse are the membranes of pre- and post-synaptic neurons in very close proximity, allowing electrical current to flow directly from one neuron to the other?
questions.explanation: At electrical synapses, the membranes of pre- and post-synaptic neurons are in very close proximity. Electrical current can flow directly from one neuron into the other across these synapses.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which statement is true regarding the speed of impulse transmission across synapses?
options: Transmission across a chemical synapse is faster than an electrical synapse.
Transmission across an electrical synapse is always faster than across a chemical synapse.
Both synapses transmit impulses at the exact same speed.
Synaptic cleft width determines the speed, making chemical synapses faster.
questions.explanation: Impulse transmission across an electrical synapse is always faster than that across a chemical synapse.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: At a chemical synapse, the gap separating the pre- and post-synaptic neurons is called the:
questions.explanation: At a chemical synapse, the membranes of the pre- and post-synaptic neurons are separated by a fluid-filled space called synaptic cleft.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: When an action potential arrives at the axon terminal, it stimulates the movement of synaptic vesicles, which then fuse with the plasma membrane to release:
questions.explanation: When an impulse (action potential) arrives at the axon terminal, it stimulates the movement of the synaptic vesicles towards the membrane where they fuse with the plasma membrane and release their neurotransmitters in the synaptic cleft.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: After neurotransmitters are released into the synaptic cleft, they bind to specific receptors present on the:
questions.explanation: The released neurotransmitters bind to their specific receptors, present on the post-synaptic membrane.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The brain is protected by cranial meninges. What is the outermost layer of these meninges called?
questions.explanation: Inside the skull, the brain is covered by cranial meninges consisting of an outer layer called dura mater...
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which layer of the cranial meninges is in direct contact with the brain tissue?
questions.explanation: ...a very thin middle layer called arachnoid and an inner layer (which is in contact with the brain tissue) called pia mater.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The brain can be divided into three major parts. Which of the following is NOT one of these parts?
questions.explanation: The brain can be divided into three major parts: (i) forebrain, (ii) midbrain, and (iii) hindbrain.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The forebrain consists of three main structures: the cerebrum, the hypothalamus, and the:
questions.explanation: The forebrain consists of cerebrum, thalamus and hypothalamus.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The left and right cerebral hemispheres are connected by a tract of nerve fibres called the:
questions.explanation: The hemispheres are connected by a tract of nerve fibres called corpus callosum.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The layer of cells covering the cerebral hemisphere is called the cerebral cortex. It is referred to as the 'grey matter' due to the concentration of:
questions.explanation: The cerebral cortex is referred to as the grey matter due to its greyish appearance. The neuron cell bodies are concentrated here giving the colour.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In the cerebral cortex, the regions that are neither clearly sensory nor motor in function and are responsible for complex functions like memory and communication are called:
questions.explanation: These regions called as the association areas are responsible for complex functions like intersensory associations, memory and communication.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The inner part of the cerebral hemisphere is called the 'white matter' because it contains fibres covered with:
questions.explanation: Fibres of the tracts are covered with the myelin sheath, which constitute the inner part of cerebral hemisphere. They give an opaque white appearance to the layer and, hence, is called the white matter.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which part of the forebrain wraps around the thalamus and acts as a major coordinating centre for sensory and motor signaling?
questions.explanation: The cerebrum wraps around a structure called thalamus, which is a major coordinating centre for sensory and motor signaling.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which structure lies at the base of the thalamus and contains centres that control body temperature, urge for eating, and drinking?
questions.explanation: Another very important part of the brain called hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus. The hypothalamus contains a number of centres which control body temperature, urge for eating and drinking.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The inner parts of the cerebral hemispheres and associated deep structures like the amygdala and hippocampus form a complex structure called the:
questions.explanation: The inner parts of cerebral hemispheres and a group of associated deep structures like amygdala, hippocampus, etc., form a complex structure called the limbic lobe or limbic system.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Along with the hypothalamus, the limbic system is involved in the regulation of:
options: Sexual behaviour, emotional reactions, and motivation
questions.explanation: Along with the hypothalamus, it [limbic system] is involved in the regulation of sexual behaviour, expression of emotional reactions (e.g., excitement, pleasure, rage and fear), and motivation.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The midbrain is located between the pons of the hindbrain and the:
questions.explanation: The midbrain is located between the thalamus/hypothalamus of the forebrain and pons of the hindbrain.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the canal that passes through the midbrain called?
questions.explanation: A canal called the cerebral aqueduct passess through the midbrain.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The dorsal portion of the midbrain consists mainly of four round swellings (lobes) known as:
questions.explanation: The dorsal portion of the midbrain consists mainly of four round swellings (lobes) called corpora quadrigemina.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The hindbrain comprises three structures: the medulla, the cerebellum, and the:
questions.explanation: The hindbrain comprises pons, cerebellum and medulla (also called the medulla oblongata).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which part of the hindbrain consists of fibre tracts that interconnect different regions of the brain?
questions.explanation: Pons consists of fibre tracts that interconnect different regions of the brain.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Why does the cerebellum have a very convoluted surface?
options: To provide additional space for many more neurons
To protect the delicate inner brain tissues
To connect directly with the spinal cord
questions.explanation: Cerebellum has very convoluted surface in order to provide the additional space for many more neurons.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which part of the brain is directly connected to the spinal cord?
questions.explanation: The medulla of the brain is connected to the spinal cord.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The medulla contains centres that control which of the following involuntary functions?
options: Respiration, cardiovascular reflexes, and gastric secretions
questions.explanation: The medulla contains centres which control respiration, cardiovascular reflexes and gastric secretions.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which three major regions make up the brain stem?
questions.explanation: Three major regions make up the brain stem; mid brain, pons and medulla oblongata.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the primary function of the brain stem?
options: It processes complex thoughts and memory.
It regulates body temperature and eating urges.
It forms the connections between the brain and the spinal cord.
It acts as the primary visual processing center.
questions.explanation: Brain stem forms the connections between the brain and spinal cord.
questions.subject: Biology
